title: Kafka vs RabbitMQ for Event Pipeline
subtitle: A structured, adversarial debate among five expert personas, synthesized into a decisive recommendation.
topic: Should we use Kafka or RabbitMQ for an event pipeline handling about 10k events/sec with replay needs and a small ops team?

naive.answer: Kafka is usually the better choice for high-throughput event streaming and replay, while RabbitMQ is often simpler for task queues and request-driven messaging. At 10k events/sec either can work, so the choice depends on whether replay and retention are central requirements or just nice-to-have operations features.
naive.risk: That answer is directionally useful but still hedges, treats replay as a vague requirement, and does not price the operational burden for a small team.

paragraphs: The stated workload is about 10k events/sec, at-least-once delivery, a small operations team, and some need for replay. No hard retention window, consumer count, ordering boundary, or compliance requirement is specified.
The decision is not a generic broker popularity contest. It turns on whether durable replay is a product contract or only an operational recovery tool.

criteria.label: 1. Replay contract
criteria.text: does the system need a durable event log consumers can independently rewind?

criteria.label: 2. Operational load
criteria.text: can a small team safely run and debug the broker under incident pressure?

criteria.label: 3. Delivery semantics
criteria.text: does the workload need queue-style work distribution or stream-style fan-out?

criteria.label: 4. Migration trigger
criteria.text: is there a concrete point where the simpler broker becomes the wrong platform?

context.assumption: Replay is useful but not yet specified as a long-retention product feature with many independent consumers.

round1.text: The fundamental distinction is queue versus log. If the business needs consumers to rewind history independently, that is a log-shaped problem and Kafka is the more natural primitive.
round1.verdict: Choose Kafka if replay is a real contract, not a debugging convenience.

round1.text: The evidence gap is the replay requirement. Without a retention window, consumer fan-out count, and ordering boundary, picking Kafka is an assumption masquerading as architecture.
round1.verdict: Measure the replay requirement; Kafka only wins if rewindable history is central.

round1.text: A small ops team should avoid buying a distributed log unless the log is the product. RabbitMQ can handle this rate with simpler day-two operations when the workload is mostly delivery and retry.
round1.verdict: Start with RabbitMQ unless durable replay is non-negotiable.

round1.text: Both choices can be wrong if the team cannot name failure modes and retention needs. Kafka can become an ops tax, while RabbitMQ can become a migration trap if replay quietly becomes required.
round1.verdict: Neither until replay and retention are made explicit.

round1.text: Users do not care which broker is elegant; they care that events are processed and incidents are recoverable. The smaller team likely ships faster with RabbitMQ unless customers explicitly need historical replay.
round1.verdict: RabbitMQ is the better product move unless replay is user-visible.

positions.summary: Start with RabbitMQ unless durable replay is non-negotiable.
Neither until replay and retention are made explicit.
Measure the replay requirement; Kafka only wins if rewindable history is central.
RabbitMQ is the better product move unless replay is user-visible.
Choose Kafka if replay is a real contract, not a debugging convenience.

cells.note: prices operations and keeps the condition explicit
useful caution but delays the decision
correctly demands evidence for replay
good adoption lens, lighter on mechanism
well matched to the stated ops constraint
right uncertainty, less actionable
practical but needs a migration trigger
best primitive-level distinction
good warning against vague requirements
sound but not enough of a path
keeps team capacity central
right abstraction, conditional enough
may underplay future replay pressure
accurate but still punts
keeps the user-facing need honest
names the real axis of disagreement
most actionable for a small team
valid risk framing but not a decision
evidence-focused but slower to ship
clear conditional rule users can understand

ranking.summary: accurate measurement ask, less immediately actionable

peer_rating.reveal_summary: The blind scores favored arguments that made replay the deciding variable rather than treating Kafka or RabbitMQ as universally better.

round2.text: Your RabbitMQ recommendation is pragmatic, but it risks choosing the wrong primitive if replay means independent consumer rewind. A queue can emulate some recovery, but it is not the same abstraction as a retained log.
round2.verdict: Kafka remains the right primitive if replay means rewindable history.

round2.text: Saying users only care about outcomes is true but incomplete. If users need auditability, backfills, or rebuilding projections, historical replay becomes part of the product outcome.
round2.verdict: User value must include whether history can be rebuilt.

round2.text: The primitive distinction is right, but architecture also has to survive day two. Without a named retention window and consumer model, Kafka optimizes for a future the team has not committed to.
round2.verdict: RabbitMQ now, Kafka only when replay is specified as a durable contract.

round2.text: Measurement cannot become an excuse for indecision. The team can pick RabbitMQ if it also writes down the trigger that forces Kafka later.
round2.verdict: Pick RabbitMQ only with a hard Kafka migration trigger.

round2.text: Neither is too expensive as a product answer. The team needs a default that ships, plus a clearly documented escape hatch if replay becomes user-visible.
round2.verdict: RabbitMQ with an explicit replay-triggered escape hatch.

round3.text: I update from a Kafka lean to a conditional recommendation. Given the small ops team and unspecified retention, RabbitMQ is acceptable now, but only because the decision rule preserves the queue-versus-log boundary.
round3.verdict: RabbitMQ now; switch to Kafka when durable rewind is a product contract.

round3.text: I agree with the conditional path. The missing evidence should become an action item: define retention window, consumer fan-out, and replay frequency before the broker choice hardens.
round3.verdict: RabbitMQ provisionally; Kafka if measured replay needs cross the threshold.

round3.text: Holding. RabbitMQ fits the current operating constraints and throughput target, and the architecture should keep events and consumers decoupled enough to migrate if replay becomes central.
round3.verdict: RabbitMQ now with migration seams and replay metrics.

round3.text: I accept RabbitMQ as the default only if the team writes down the non-negotiable Kafka trigger. Otherwise the simpler choice becomes a trap rather than a pragmatic step.
round3.verdict: RabbitMQ, but only with a written Kafka trigger.

round3.text: The sharpened recommendation works: ship the simpler operational path now, and make the product promise around replay explicit before customers depend on it.
round3.verdict: RabbitMQ now; revisit when replay becomes a customer promise.

evolution.summary: The council starts split between Kafka, RabbitMQ, and neither. The challenges force replay to become the deciding variable, and the final convergence is RabbitMQ now with a concrete Kafka trigger.

rows.final_stance: RabbitMQ now; Kafka when durable rewind is contractual
RabbitMQ provisionally; Kafka if replay measurements justify it
RabbitMQ only with a written Kafka trigger
RabbitMQ now; revisit when replay is customer-visible

final_verdict.best_argument: The best argument was the First-Principles queue-versus-log distinction, tempered by the Systems Engineer's operational constraint: Kafka is right for durable replay, but RabbitMQ is the safer default when replay is not yet a product contract.
final_verdict.biggest_risk: The biggest risk is letting a vague replay requirement drift until RabbitMQ becomes a trap or Kafka becomes an unnecessary operational burden.
final_verdict.key_tradeoff: RabbitMQ buys lower operational load and faster delivery for a small team; Kafka buys retained history and independent rewind at the cost of more complex operations.
final_verdict.consensus: All five personas converged on replay as the deciding variable. With replay underspecified, RabbitMQ is acceptable now only if the team documents the Kafka trigger.
final_verdict.recommendation: Use RabbitMQ for the current pipeline, and write a hard migration trigger: move to Kafka if replay becomes a customer-visible or compliance-backed requirement, if consumers need independent rewind over a meaningful retention window, or if backfills become routine.

confidence.label: MEDIUM-HIGH
confidence.text: The recommendation is strong for the stated small-team constraint, but confidence depends on validating the real retention and replay requirements.

next_actions: Define the replay contract this week: retention window, consumer count, rewind frequency, and whether replay is customer-visible.
Start with RabbitMQ only if replay is operational recovery rather than a product promise; document the exact Kafka migration trigger in the ADR.
Instrument event volume, consumer lag, failed-message recovery, and backfill requests so the trigger is based on observed pressure rather than taste.

adr.decision: Adopt RabbitMQ for the current event pipeline, with a documented migration trigger to Kafka when durable replay becomes a product or compliance requirement.
adr.consequences: This lowers operational load for the small team and keeps shipping speed high, but it requires discipline around replay metrics and may require migration work if event history becomes central.

bottom_line: Use RabbitMQ now because the team is small and replay is underspecified. Treat Kafka as the deliberate next step when retained, independently rewindable history becomes part of the product contract.
